doc.title: Investing and financing sections
doc.event: Moss Co. this year: sold equipment (cost $50,000, accumulated depreciation $35,000) at a $10,000 gain; bought equipment for $80,000; issued common stock for $30,000; repaid a $25,000 note; paid $20,000 of dividends. Prepare the investing and financing sections.

variants.label: Reconstruct the cash from the account changes

computationPaths.narration: INVESTING: proceeds from the equipment sale must be reconstructed — book value (50,000 − 35,000 = 15,000) plus the 10,000 gain = 25,000 of cash in. That's exactly why the indirect operating section removed the gain: its cash is HERE.
FINANCING: capital in, capital out, dividends paid.

memorize.body: Sale proceeds = book value ± gain/loss. T-account every long-term account: whatever change isn't explained by noncash activity (depreciation, conversions) must be cash — that's the whole worksheet method in one sentence.
Dividends PAID (financing) ≠ dividends DECLARED — if a dividends payable balance changed, cash paid = declared ± the payable change. Same conversion discipline as the direct method.

questions.prompt: Equipment (cost 50,000, accumulated depreciation 35,000) is sold at a 10,000 gain. Investing-section cash inflow =

distractors.feedback: 10,000 is the GAIN, not the cash. Proceeds = book value 15,000 + gain 10,000 = 25,000 — and the gain's removal from operating exists precisely so this 25,000 counts once.
15,000 is the book value. The buyer paid book value PLUS the gain: 25,000 in cash.
